utterances: Get out.
After the team finally disposes of the body, they head to the exit where they're sprayed off with a chlorine solution.
Taking off the protective gear also must be done carefully to make sure there's no exposure to the virus.
Dr. Patrick Lahai Kamara, one of the trainees, is soaked in sweat as he steps out of the mock treatment unit.
It's not easy.
It's very hot.
Little by little, we'll get used to it.
Dr.
Kamara just graduated from medical school last December.
He's been working at Redemption Hospital here in Monrovia, but Redemption hasn't been treating Ebola cases.
Despite the fact that almost a hundred health care workers in Liberia have died during this outbreak, Kamara says he feels an obligation to treat Ebola patients.

Nobody's happy about what happened with the doctors that have died, but it's just like the soldiers - whenever there is war in a country, the first people you think that would go and fight for their nation is the soldiers.
So in time of health crisis, I think we, the physician, we should be the one on the front line.
If something goes wrong, he says, well, at least he died fighting a just cause.
As more and more Ebola treatment units are built, Liberia needs to find thousands of other workers who are also willing to take that risk.
Jason Beaubien, NPR News, Monrovia.
The political split in Ukraine is dividing families, too.
One couple, married nearly half a century, has hardly stopped arguing since the demonstrations in Kiev began last November.
NPR's Emily Harris visited them at their home in Kharkiv, close to Ukraine's border with Russia.
Up a creaky elevator and down a short hall, Raisa Bartozhuk and Ivan Moroz are warm and welcoming.
(Foreign language spoken)
In their cozy kitchen, she pours tea to go with homemade cheese blintzes.
(SOUNDBITE OF POURING)
Raisa's parents grew up on land that's now in Poland, forced to move 500 miles east after World War II.
They worked on a collective farm for a few years before finding their way back to western Ukraine.
She grew up there.
Her husband, Ivan, grew up in Belgorod, Russia, just an hour's drive from their home here in Kharkiv.
(Through translator) I'm Russian.
She's a western Ukrainian girl.
I'm from here.
She's from there.
They met in college at the University of Lvov in western Ukraine, although Ivan got a nasty surprise when he first arrived there.
(Through translator) I thought that everywhere in the Soviet Union, you could study in Russian.
Not in the Journalism Department at the University of Lvov, it turned out.
So, Ivan wound up studying law instead.
He says it's his legal training that makes him so mad that protests forced Ukrainian President Viktor Yanukovych from office three weeks ago.
(Through translator) They should have removed him, OK.
But they should have done it legally.
Raisa, a French teacher, disagrees.
(Through translator) They did it legally.
(Through translator) What kind of legal?
(Through translator) Parliament passed a law.
(Through translator) I've never known a president who wasn't a thief.
I'm not sure the ones in power now aren't thieves.
And today, we'll hear from Barack Obama's energy adviser, his name is Elgie Holstein.
When I spoke earlier with him, I asked Elgie Holstein to explain the differences between the two candidate's energy policies.
Senator McCain's proposals are swiftly out of proportion to the scale of the problem we have to confront in this country.
We have an extremely serious energy problem and a related problem with global climate change.
Senator McCain says, he's going to support a variety of different renewable energy proposals, and yet he's voted against them consistently in the past.
And he's proposing a freeze on spending, that would mean that you couldn't put anymore research and development into those kinds of programs.
Which are exactly the kinds of things we need to free ourselves from dependents on foreign oil.
Senator McCain has proposed that we get about the business of building 45 new nuclear power plants, not mentioning that that will put taxpayers at risk for something like $300 billion.
Well, if we've got that kind of money to spend we probably ought to be putting it into the kinds of things that we know work.
Such as energy efficiency, renewable energy and yes, the development of our domestic resources like clean coal technologies.
I'm wondering, given the current economic climate where the government is talking about spending $700 billion bailout to financial markets.
Whether or not Senator Obama's proposals are feasible, given the fact that if he is president, he'll have to take a serious look at spending money and spending money on this proposals.
I believed he has pledge $150 billion over ten years for the development of alternative energy.
Yes, he has and he's explained exactly where that money would come from.
The solution is going to involve at least impart the auctioning of permits to polluters.
So that if they have to continue to burn dirty fuels that contribute to greenhouse gases, they'll have to buy permits in order to do that.

The result of this permit auctioning process is that the government takes in roughly a $100 dollars a year, which can be used for Energy Research and Development and for helping companies afford the transition to cleaner equipment.
Auctioning of permits is part of the cap and trade proposal?
Yes, it's part of the cap and trade proposal for attacking the global climate change problem.
Is Senator Obama worried at all that his proposal during these hard economic times, his proposals not only for cap and trade but for higher emission standards by the U.S.
auto industry and higher taxes - windfall profit taxes - for the oil and gas industries?
Is he afraid at all that this is going to put a damper on business at a time when we're headed into a recession?
The answer to that question depends largely upon how you phase in the cap and trade system.
If it's done gradually, so that companies have plenty of time to plan.
They can decide on their own without government mandate, exactly the best was to meet the emission reduction's targets that will be necessary to avoid catastrophic changes in our atmosphere.
Let's talk about off-shore drilling, Senator Obama early in the campaign was against it.
In the summer when gas prices were very high, he softened his position and said he would favor some off-shore oil drilling.
What do you say to some environmentalists who were disappointed by that, who say, you know, he changed his mind for political expediency?
Senator Obama has always supported drilling in the offshore areas, in those areas that have been opened to drilling, exploration and production.
What he has said over the summer is that he'd be willing to consider a bipartisan effort in Congress around the idea that you could do some additional off-shore drilling.

And some of those places, for example, off of the Georgia or Carolina coast lines where provided the states agreed to it and subject to very close environmental regulation, you could do some additional leasing there.

But only if, it were part of an overall or comprehensive package that included investments in energy research and development, renewable energy sources, and of course, fair tax treatment to the oil industry.
So taken all of Senator Obama's proposals and plans, where do you see, let say in 10 years, America's dependence on foreign oil?
Well, this is clearly something we have to turn around.
And if in 10 years, we expect to see a substantial reduction or elimination of the oil that we import.
We are going to have to make the kind of investment and national commitment that was typical for example of the man on the moon objectives that were set back in the 1960's.
It needs to be that scale of commitment.
Elgie Holstein is Senator Barack Obama's Energy adviser, Elgie.
Thank you very much.
Madeleine, thank you.
My pleasure.
(Soundbite of music)
Stay with us, NPR's Day to Day continues.
An update now from eastern Ukraine, where, for the last three years, Russia has fueled a separatist war.
Vladimir Putin insists he wants only to protect ethnic Russians there.
He hasn't annexed the region as he did Crimea.
And Russia hasn't even officially recognized the independence of Ukraine's breakaway regions.
But Russia is where the sympathies of many people there lie.
NPR's Soraya Sarhaddi Nelson explains why.
There's little sign of the war in the capital of what anti-Ukrainian forces call the Donetsk People's Republic or DPR.
The main streets and facades in the city of Donetsk look pristine, if empty, given that many people who prefer Donetsk stay a part of Ukraine have moved away.
(SOUNDBITE OF CONSTRUCTION NOISE)
The municipal workers are nevertheless out in force, filling potholes and making repairs.
It's my first time in the self-proclaimed republic in nearly three years because American journalists are rarely allowed inside.
Just how normal everything looks here is striking.
DPR officials say credit goes to the Russian Federation, which provides 80 percent of the material they need to keep services running.
Well, let's ask NPR political editor Domenico Montanaro.
Good morning, Domenico.
Hey, there.
Before the early 1960s, maps of this area were notable for their inconsistency.
One would place Ghajar in the Syrian Golan Heights, another in Lebanon.

But on the ground, Kaufman says, the Alawite villagers considered themselves Syrian, and Lebanon seemed to lay no claim to Ghajar; until the early 1960s, when this area became part of the so-called water wars among Lebanon, Syria, Jordan and Israel.
The U.S.
ambassador to Lebanon tried to sort out sovereignty issues in and around Ghajar.
And he concluded, in a document found by Kaufman at the U.S.
archives, that Lebanese mapping efforts were amateurish and that the reality on the ground was that Ghajar was part of Syria.
In 1963, says Kaufman, after the U.S.
ambassador's comments, Lebanon's laissez-faire attitude toward Ghajar seemed to change.
Because in 1963, we have, for the first time, a Lebanese map that depicts this new reality, where the village of Ghajar all of a sudden is divided into two sections.
So a new reality is now being produced.
